AQA
GCSE
ECONOMICS
8136/1
Paper 1 How Markets Work

Mark scheme

Predicted Paper — Advanced
Version: 1.0 Final


Mark schemes are prepared by the Lead Assessment Writer and considered, together with the relevant
questions, by a panel of subject teachers.

This mark scheme is for a predicted paper in the style of AQA GCSE Economics 8136/1 (How Markets
Work). The format, command words, mark allocation and Assessment Objectives mirror the June 2024
paper.

It must be stressed that a mark scheme is a working document. Indicative content is provided as a guide for
examiners. It is not intended to be exhaustive and other valid points should be credited. Students do not
have to cover all of the points mentioned in the Indicative content to reach the highest level of the mark
scheme.

An answer which contains nothing of relevance to the question must be awarded no marks.
                                                    MARK SCHEME – GCSE ECONOMICS – 8136/1 – PREDICTED

                                                                                                            marks

  11               State two forms of non-price competition that firms may use.                                2

                   AO1 = 2

                   1 mark for each valid form (max 2).

                   Possible answers:
                    • advertising and marketing
                    • branding / brand identity
                    • product differentiation (different features or styles)
                    • improving product quality
                    • improving customer service / after-sales service
                    • loyalty schemes and reward points
                    • new / innovative product features
                   Note: This indicative content is not exhaustive, other creditworthy responses should be awarded
                   marks as appropriate.


IB/H/Pred/8136/1                                                                                                     2
 Qu     Part       Marking guidance                                                                         Total
                                                                                                            marks

  12               Explain one possible impact of a highly competitive market on the efficiency                2
                   of firms.

                   AO1 = 2

                    Level    Marks     Description

                      2         2      Possible impact on efficiency explained.

                      1         1      Possible impact on efficiency identified.

                                0      Nothing written worthy of credit.

                   Possible answers:
                    • firms must minimise costs to compete on price → productive efficiency rises
                    • inefficient firms make losses and exit the market → average efficiency of the
                      industry improves
                    • pressure to innovate / improve productivity to keep up with rivals
                    • firms must respond quickly to consumer demands → allocative efficiency improves
                   Note: This indicative content is not exhaustive, other creditworthy responses should be awarded
                   marks as appropriate.


 Qu     Part       Marking guidance                                                                         Total
                                                                                                            marks

  13               Explain why the foreign exchange (forex) market is an example of a market.                  2

                   AO1 = 2

                    Level    Marks     Description

                      2         2      Reason explained.

                      1         1      Reason identified.

                                0      Nothing written worthy of credit.

                   Possible answers:
                    • buyers (those wanting a foreign currency) and sellers (those wanting to sell it) come
                      into contact
                    • there is demand (e.g. from importers / tourists / investors) and supply (e.g. from
                      exporters / central banks) of currencies
                    • the exchange rate (price) is determined by supply and demand for each currency
                   Note: This indicative content is not exhaustive, other creditworthy responses should be awarded
                   marks as appropriate.


IB/H/Pred/8136/1                                                                                                     3
                                                    MARK SCHEME – GCSE ECONOMICS – 8136/1 – PREDICTED


 Qu     Part       Marking guidance                                                                         Total
                                                                                                            marks

  14      1        Calculate the firm's profit margin to the nearest whole %.                                  2

                   AO2 = 2
                    • Total revenue = £108 000
                    • Total variable costs = £24 × 1 200 = £28 800
                    • Total costs = £28 000 + £28 800 = £56 800
                    • Profit = £108 000 – £56 800 = £51 200
                    • Profit margin = £51 200 ÷ £108 000 × 100 = 47.4% ≈ 47%

                   1 mark for correct method with correct figures but incorrect final answer.

                   1 mark for a correctly calculated profit figure (£51 200) but incorrect margin.

                   2 marks for a correct answer with no working shown.


 Qu     Part       Marking guidance                                                                         Total
                                                                                                            marks

  14      2        Explain one way the software firm could increase its profit margin.                         2

                   AO1 = 2

                    Level    Marks     Description

                      2         2      Possible way explained.

                      1         1      Possible way identified.

                                0      Nothing written worthy of credit.

                   Possible answers:
                    • raise the subscription price (provided demand is inelastic)
                    • cut variable costs (e.g. cheaper cloud-hosting, automation)
                    • cut fixed costs (e.g. reduced office space, remote working)
                    • increase sales at the same cost base (spreading fixed costs)
                    • introduce a higher-priced premium tier
                    • focus on retention to reduce customer-acquisition costs
                   Note: This indicative content is not exhaustive, other creditworthy responses should be awarded
                   marks as appropriate.


IB/H/Pred/8136/1                                                                                                     4
 Qu     Part       Marking guidance                                                                         Total
                                                                                                            marks

  15               Analyse the possible impact of rising input costs such as those shown in                    6
                   Figure 1 upon UK manufacturing firms and the markets in which they operate.

                   AO2 = 2, AO3 = 4

                    Level    Marks     Description

                      3        5–6     A full analysis of the economic evidence, a well-developed and logical
                                       chain of reasoning.

                      2        3–4     An incomplete analysis of the economic evidence, starts to develop a
                                       chain of reasoning. Good application of knowledge and understanding
                                       of concepts/issues mostly relevant to the context.

                      1        1–2     Analysis of discrete points of the economic evidence with no chain of
                                       reasoning attempted. Basic application of knowledge and
                                       understanding of concepts/issues with limited relevance to the context.

                                0      Nothing written worthy of credit.

                   Indicative content:
                    • higher input costs shift the supply curve to the left → higher equilibrium prices in
                      output markets
                    • firms face reduced profit margins if unable to pass costs on
                    • firms may cut production or lay off staff to reduce costs → possible unemployment
                    • smaller or less well-capitalised firms may exit the market → reduced competition in
                      the long run
                    • input costs may be passed on as higher prices → contributing to cost-push inflation
                    • consumers may switch to substitutes or imports → reduced UK market share
                    • firms may respond by investing in efficiency / alternative inputs / automation in the
                      longer run
                    • reference to data: gas prices rose 141%, electricity 86% — extreme increases
                      concentrated in energy-intensive sectors (steel, chemicals)
                   Note: This indicative content is not exhaustive, other creditworthy responses should be awarded
                   marks as appropriate.


IB/H/Pred/8136/1                                                                                                     5
                                                    MARK SCHEME – GCSE ECONOMICS – 8136/1 – PREDICTED


 Qu     Part       Marking guidance                                                                         Total
                                                                                                            marks

  16               State two possible barriers to entry that firms in an industry might face.                  2

                   AO1 = 2

                   1 mark for each valid barrier (max 2).

                   Possible answers:
                    • high start-up / fixed costs
                    • economies of scale enjoyed by established firms
                    • strong brand loyalty of existing firms
                    • patents, copyrights and licences
                    • control of essential inputs / distribution by incumbents
                    • government regulations / licensing requirements
                    • predatory pricing by incumbents
                   Note: This indicative content is not exhaustive, other creditworthy responses should be awarded
                   marks as appropriate.


 Qu     Part       Marking guidance                                                                         Total
                                                                                                            marks

  17               Explain one possible reason why investment bankers in the City of London                    2
                   tend to earn significantly more than secondary school teachers.

                   AO1 = 2

                    Level    Marks     Description

                      2         2      Possible reason explained.

                      1         1      Possible reason identified.

                                0      Nothing written worthy of credit.

                   Possible answers:
                    • investment banking requires highly specialised quantitative/financial skills in limited
                      supply
                    • banks compete globally for top talent → wages bid upwards
                    • bankers' output is linked to very large financial transactions, so their marginal
                      revenue product is extremely high
                    • teachers are relatively numerous; training pathways are well established → larger
                      labour supply
                    • the government (monopsony) largely determines teacher pay, constraining wage
                      rises
                    • long, unsocial hours and stress in banking attract compensating wage differentials
                   Note: This indicative content is not exhaustive, other creditworthy responses should be awarded
                   marks as appropriate.


IB/H/Pred/8136/1                                                                                                     6
 Qu     Part       Marking guidance                                                                         Total
                                                                                                            marks

  18               Explain one possible reason why the demand for a life-saving prescription                   2
                   drug such as insulin is likely to be price inelastic.

                   AO1 = 2

                    Level    Marks     Description

                      2         2      Possible reason explained.

                      1         1      Possible reason identified.

                                0      Nothing written worthy of credit.

                   Possible answers:
                    • insulin is a necessity — patients cannot survive long without it, so they will pay
                      almost any price
                    • there are no or very few close substitutes for the specific drug
                    • the decision to use insulin is driven by medical need, not price
                    • many patients' costs are covered by the NHS or insurance → they are not price-
                      sensitive at point of use
                    • a clear, general explanation of 'price inelastic' without reference to insulin = 1 mark
                   Note: This indicative content is not exhaustive, other creditworthy responses should be awarded
                   marks as appropriate.


IB/H/Pred/8136/1                                                                                                     7
                                                    MARK SCHEME – GCSE ECONOMICS – 8136/1 – PREDICTED


 Qu     Part       Marking guidance                                                                         Total
                                                                                                            marks

  19      1        Using Table 3, calculate the monthly productivity of an analyst at Ansel's                  2
                   Analytics. Give your answer to one decimal place.

                   AO2 = 2
                    • 203 ÷ 14 = 14.5 reports per analyst per month

                   2 marks for correct answer with method.

                   1 mark for correct method but incorrect answer.

                   2 marks for a correct answer (14.5) with no working shown.


 Qu     Part       Marking guidance                                                                         Total
                                                                                                            marks

  19      2        Explain one benefit of increased labour productivity to a business such as                  2
                   Ansel's Analytics.

                   AO1 = 2

                    Level    Marks     Description

                      2         2      Possible benefit explained.

                      1         1      Possible benefit identified.

                                0      Nothing written worthy of credit.

                   Possible answers:
                    • lower unit labour cost → greater margin on each client report
                    • higher margin allows more competitive pricing or higher profits
                    • ability to serve more clients without hiring more staff
                    • potential to pay higher wages, aiding retention of skilled analysts
                    • improved reputation for speed / reliability → more new clients
                   Note: This indicative content is not exhaustive, other creditworthy responses should be awarded
                   marks as appropriate.


IB/H/Pred/8136/1                                                                                                     8
 Qu     Part       Marking guidance                                                                Total
                                                                                                   marks

  20               Draw and label demand and supply curves on the graph from the table.               3
                   Clearly identify the equilibrium price and quantity.

                   AO2 = 3
                    • 1 mark for correct drawing and labelling of the demand curve.
                    • 1 mark for correct drawing and labelling of the supply curve.
                    • 1 mark for correctly identifying the equilibrium price £30 and equilibrium quantity
                      30 units.


IB/H/Pred/8136/1                                                                                            9
                   MARK SCHEME – GCSE ECONOMICS – 8136/1 – PREDICTED


IB/H/Pred/8136/1                                                  10
 Qu     Part       Marking guidance                                                                      Total
                                                                                                         marks

  21               Using Figure 2 and Table 4, assess whether the benefits of growth for a                  9
                   business such as TechCo Solutions continue to exceed the costs as it
                   becomes very large.

                   AO2 = 3, AO3 = 6

                    Level   Marks     Description

                      3      7–9      A fully justified conclusion with sustained evaluation, coherent and
                                      relevant, with judgements supported by evidence. A full analysis of the
                                      economic evidence, a well-developed and logical chain of reasoning.
                                      Comprehensive application of knowledge and understanding of
                                      pertinent concepts/issues relevant to the context; specialist language
                                      used throughout.

                      2      4–6      A partially justified conclusion with an evaluation, mostly coherent and
                                      relevant; judgements may not be fully supported by evidence. An
                                      incomplete analysis of the economic evidence; starts to develop a
                                      chain of reasoning. Good application of knowledge and understanding
                                      of concepts/issues mostly relevant to the context; specialist language
                                      used mostly appropriately.

                      1      1–3      A basic conclusion or assertion may be present based on evaluative
                                      judgements supported by little or no evidence. Analysis of discrete
                                      points of the economic evidence with no chain of reasoning attempted.
                                      Basic application of knowledge and understanding of concepts/issues
                                      with limited relevance to the context; may use non-specialist language.

                               0      Nothing written worthy of credit.

                   Indicative content:
                    • Benefits (economies of scale): very substantial reduction in average cost per
                      licence at first
                    • technical economies — servers and automated testing have high fixed cost but low
                      marginal cost
                    • research-and-development is affordable at scale only (mentioned in Figure 2)
                    • financial economies — lower borrowing costs for larger firms
                    • risk-bearing economies — diversification across four markets
                    • Data: AC at 5k = £250; AC at 10k = £210; AC at 20k = £180 → clear fall (economies
                      of scale)
                    • Costs (diseconomies of scale): communication and coordination problems
                      between four offices (Figure 2)
                    • loss of common company culture → possible weaker motivation / retention
                    • decision-making becomes slower as the firm scales
                    • Data: AC at 30k = £230; AC at 40k = £280 → costs rising sharply (diseconomies of
                      scale)
                    • Evaluation: benefits clearly exceed costs up to 20 000 licences; beyond that, costs
                      exceed benefits; optimum scale ≈ 20 000 units


IB/H/Pred/8136/1                                                                                                 11
                    • evaluation of relevance to a knowledge-based tech business (vs. a manufacturing
                      business) — scaling brings both strong technical gains and serious managerial
                      challenges
                   Note: This indicative content is not exhaustive, other creditworthy responses should be awarded
                   marks as appropriate.


IB/H/Pred/8136/1                                                                                                     12

 Qu     Part       Marking guidance                                                                         Total
                                                                                                            marks

  22               Define the term 'oligopoly'.                                                                2

                   AO1 = 2

                    Level    Marks     Description

                      2         2      Complete definition of oligopoly.

                      1         1      Partial definition of oligopoly.

                                0      Nothing written worthy of credit.

                   Possible answers:
                    • a market dominated by a small number of large firms (2 marks)
                    • a market structure in which a few firms together have a large share of total sales
                      and can influence market prices (2 marks)
                    • a market with few firms (1 mark)
                   Note: This indicative content is not exhaustive, other creditworthy responses should be awarded
                   marks as appropriate.


 Qu     Part       Marking guidance                                                                         Total
                                                                                                            marks

  23               Calculate the percentage change in the UK household video-streaming                         3
                   subscription rate between 2015 and 2023. Give your answer to the nearest
                   whole %.

                   AO2 = 3
                    • 2015 ≈ 12%; 2023 ≈ 68%
                    • (68 – 12) ÷ 12 × 100 = 466.67% ≈ 467%

                   Accept any answer between 450% and 485%, using values from acceptable range.

                   1 mark for correct method but wrong answer.

                   1 mark for correctly identifying values from the graph for both years within acceptable
                   ranges.

                   2 marks if not rounded to nearest whole %.

                   3 marks for a correct answer with no working shown.


IB/H/Pred/8136/1                                                                                                     13
                                                    MARK SCHEME – GCSE ECONOMICS – 8136/1 – PREDICTED


 Qu     Part       Marking guidance                                                                         Total
                                                                                                            marks

  24               Analyse the possible positive externalities that may result from the                        6
                   widespread availability of affordable video-streaming services.

                   AO2 = 6

                    Level    Marks     Description

                      3        5–6     A full analysis of the economic evidence, a well-developed and logical
                                       chain of reasoning.

                      2        3–4     An incomplete analysis of the economic evidence, starts to develop a
                                       chain of reasoning. Good application of knowledge and understanding
                                       of concepts/issues mostly relevant to the context.

                      1        1–2     Analysis of discrete points of the economic evidence with no chain of
                                       reasoning attempted. Basic application of knowledge and
                                       understanding of concepts/issues with limited relevance to the context.

                                0      Nothing written worthy of credit.

                   Possible answers:
                    • educational benefits — documentaries and programmes improving knowledge and
                      cultural literacy
                    • investment in UK creative industries (e.g. Netflix's UK productions) → jobs and skills
                      spillovers
                    • support for the broader UK film/TV supply chain (studios, crews, equipment hire)
                    • substitution away from physical DVDs → reduced plastic/packaging waste
                    • less travel for entertainment (cinema trips replaced) → reduced transport emissions
                    • wider accessibility of international cultural content → soft-power benefits and
                      diversity of viewpoints
                    • social benefit — shared cultural experience / shared conversations
                   Note: This indicative content is not exhaustive, other creditworthy responses should be awarded
                   marks as appropriate.


IB/H/Pred/8136/1                                                                                                     14
                                                    MARK SCHEME – GCSE ECONOMICS – 8136/1 – PREDICTED


 Qu     Part       Marking guidance                                                                         Total
                                                                                                            marks

  25               Explain two possible disadvantages, for consumers, of the UK video-                         6
                   streaming market being dominated by a small number of large firms.

                   AO2 = 2, AO3 = 4

                   Apply the following mark scheme for each disadvantage.

                    Level    Marks     Description

                      3         3      A full and precise explanation applying economic concepts and issues.

                      2         2      A partial explanation applying economic concepts and issues but
                                       lacking detail.

                      1         1      Fragmented points made.

                                0      Nothing written worthy of credit.

                   Possible answers:
                    • rising subscription prices and repeated price hikes (Netflix £8.99 → £10.99) —
                      consumers capture less consumer surplus
                    • reduced choice — large firms set the menu of available content; less niche content
                      likely to be commissioned
                    • restrictive practices (password-sharing crackdowns, ad-supported tiers) reduce
                      perceived value for money
                    • high barriers to entry mean new / challenger firms cannot easily enter to compete →
                      prolonged market power
                    • dependence on a small number of US-based firms for UK cultural content
                    • risk of exclusive content 'walling off' programmes behind multiple separate
                      subscriptions
                   Note: This indicative content is not exhaustive, other creditworthy responses should be awarded
                   marks as appropriate.


IB/H/Pred/8136/1                                                                                                     15
                   MARK SCHEME – GCSE ECONOMICS – 8136/1 – PREDICTED


IB/H/Pred/8136/1                                                  16
 Qu     Part       Marking guidance                                                                        Total
                                                                                                           marks

  26               Do you think the UK government should actively regulate the UK streaming                  15
                   market — for example, by capping prices, imposing UK content quotas and
                   subsidising smaller UK-based platforms? Use Items A and B and your own
                   economic knowledge to justify your view.

                   AO2 = 5, AO3 = 10

                    Level   Marks     Description

                      5     13–15     A justified conclusion with sustained evaluation, coherent and relevant,
                                      with judgements fully supported by evidence. Analysis of a relevant
                                      selection of the economic evidence, with a well-developed logical
                                      chain of reasoning. Comprehensive application of knowledge and
                                      understanding of pertinent concepts/issues relevant to the context;
                                      effective use of specialist language maintained throughout.

                      4     10–12     A partially justified conclusion with an evaluation, mostly coherent and
                                      relevant; judgements may not be fully supported by evidence. Analysis
                                      of a relevant selection of the economic evidence; a logical chain of
                                      reasoning established but not fully developed. Strong application of
                                      knowledge and understanding of a broad range of concepts/issues
                                      relevant to the context; mostly effective use of specialist language
                                      throughout.

                      3      7–9      A partially justified conclusion with an evaluation, but not integrated in
                                      a coherent way; judgements are not fully supported by evidence.
                                      Analysis of a relevant selection of the economic evidence and starts to
                                      develop a chain of reasoning. Good application of knowledge and
                                      understanding of a broad range of concepts/issues relevant to the
                                      context; consistent use of specialist language but not always effective.

                      2      4–6      A basic conclusion is present based on simple evaluative judgements
                                      supported by little or no evidence. Analysis of a relevant selection of
                                      the economic evidence with no chain of reasoning attempted. Simple
                                      application of knowledge and understanding of a narrow range of
                                      concepts/issues relevant to the context; sporadic use of specialist
                                      language.

                      1      1–3      Evaluative judgements are simple with no conclusion present. Analysis
                                      of a limited selection of the economic evidence with no chain of
                                      reasoning attempted; selection of evidence may lack relevance. Basic
                                      application of knowledge and understanding of concepts/issues with
                                      limited relevance to the context; uses non-specialist language.

                              0       Nothing written worthy of credit.

                   Indicative content:

                   Yes (in favour of regulation):

                    • classic oligopoly features (Item A) — few firms, high barriers to entry, price rises
                      without losing customers → case for intervention
                    • market failure — dominant firms capture large producer surplus at the expense of
                      consumer surplus


IB/H/Pred/8136/1                                                                                                   17
                    • UK content quotas protect cultural diversity and support the UK creative industries
                      (jobs, skills)
                    • subsidies for smaller UK platforms address barriers to entry (cost of original content,
                      Item B)
                    • price caps protect lower-income households from being priced out of the market

                   No (against regulation):

                    • price caps may reduce investment in new content (Item B)
                    • the market is highly dynamic and cancellation is easy — consumer power may
                      already be strong
                    • regulation creates compliance costs and may deter new foreign investment into UK
                      production
                    • subsidies have an opportunity cost — funds taken from the NHS, schools or
                      defence
                    • quotas risk reducing the quality of content if firms must meet arbitrary UK-production
                      targets
                    • breaking up firms may reduce economies of scale in content production

                   Overall judgement:

                    • lighter intervention (e.g. funding requirements for public-service broadcasters) may
                      be more proportionate than price caps or break-ups
                    • depends on how persistent market power proves to be as new entrants (Apple TV+,
                      Paramount+) emerge
                    • short-run consumer gains from price caps vs. long-run impact on investment in UK
                      content
                    • need to balance protecting UK creative industries with avoiding protectionist
                      regulation
                   Note: This indicative content is not exhaustive, other creditworthy responses should be awarded
                   marks as appropriate.
